statement: A \textbf{based map} $h\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ is a continuous map $h\colon X\to Y$ with $h(x_0)=y_0$.
gloss: A continuous map that respects chosen basepoints. Based maps are what induce homomorphisms of fundamental groups.
example: Composing a loop at $x_0$ with $h$ gives a loop at $y_0$.

statement: If $X$ is path-connected and $x_0,x_1\in X$, then $\pi_1(X,x_0)\cong\pi_1(X,x_1)$.
gloss: In a path-connected space the fundamental group does not depend (up to isomorphism) on the chosen basepoint — so one often writes $\pi_1(X)$.
example: A path $\alpha$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ induces the isomorphism $\hat\alpha([f])=[\alpha]^{-1}*[f]*[\alpha]$.

statement: A \textbf{basis} on $X$ is a collection $\mathcal{B}$ of subsets such that:\;\textbf{(B1)}\ every $x\in X$ lies in some $B\in\mathcal{B}$;\;\textbf{(B2)}\ if $x\in B_1\cap B_2$ with $B_1,B_2\in\mathcal{B}$, there is $B_3\in\mathcal{B}$ with $x\in B_3\subseteq B_1\cap B_2$.
gloss: A small kit of sets from which all open sets are built as unions. Open $=$ union of basis elements (Thm. 4.10).
example: The open $\epsilon$-balls form a basis for the metric topology; all singletons form a basis for the discrete topology.

statement: A \textbf{binary operation} on a set $S$ is a map $*\colon S\times S\to S$; one writes $a*b$ for $*(a,b)$.
gloss: A rule combining two elements of $S$ into a third, with $S$ closed under it. The raw ingredient of a group.
example: Addition and multiplication on $\mathbb{Z}$ are binary operations.

statement: In a metric space $(X,d)$, a subset $A$ is \textbf{bounded} if there is $M\in\mathbb{R}$ with $d(a_1,a_2)\le M$ for all $a_1,a_2\in A$.
gloss: The set fits inside some ball of finite radius — its points are never more than $M$ apart.
example: In $\mathbb{R}^n$, $A$ is bounded iff it sits inside some ball about the origin. Boundedness is metric, not topological.

statement: Every continuous map $f\colon D^n\to D^n$ of the closed unit disc to itself has a fixed point: some $x$ with $f(x)=x$. (Proved here for $n=2$ via $\pi_1(S^1)$.)
gloss: You cannot continuously map a disc to itself without leaving some point fixed. In dimension $2$ the proof uses that no retraction $D^2\to S^1$ exists.
example: If $f$ had no fixed point, the ray from $f(x)$ through $x$ would define a retraction $D^2\to S^1$, contradicting $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

statement: $A\times B=\{(x,y)\mid x\in A,\ y\in B\}$, the set of ordered pairs.
gloss: All pairings of an element of $A$ with an element of $B$ — the set underlying the product topology.
example: $\mathbb{R}^2=\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}$; the torus is $S^1\times S^1$.

statement: A subset $K$ of a topological space $X$ is \textbf{closed} if its complement $K^c=X\setminus K$ is open.
gloss: Closed = complement is open. Closed sets satisfy the dual axioms: arbitrary intersections and finite unions of closed sets are closed (Thm. 3.28).
example: In a discrete space every subset is closed; in $\mathbb{R}$ every interval $[a,b]$ and every singleton is closed.

statement: The \textbf{closure} $\overline{A}$ of $A\subseteq X$ is the intersection of all closed subsets of $X$ containing $A$ — the smallest closed set containing $A$.
gloss: The closure adds exactly the points $A$ “almost reaches.” If $A$ is already closed then $\overline{A}=A$.
example: In $\mathbb{R}$, $\overline{(a,b]}=[a,b]$. In general $\mathrm{Int}(A)\subseteq A\subseteq\overline{A}$.

statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{cofinite topology} consists of $\varnothing$ together with all $U\subseteq X$ whose complement $X\setminus U$ is finite.
gloss: A subset is open exactly when it misses only finitely many points. A natural example of a topology that need not come from a metric.
example: If $X$ is finite, the cofinite topology equals the discrete topology; if $X$ is infinite it is strictly coarser.

statement: $X$ is \textbf{compact} if every open cover of $X$ has a finite subcover.
gloss: No matter how you cover $X$ with open sets, finitely many already suffice. A powerful finiteness condition standing in for “closed and bounded.”
example: Finite spaces and indiscrete spaces are compact; $\mathbb{R}$ is not (the cover by $(n-1,n+1)$ has no finite subcover).

term: Compact subset of Hausdorff is closed
statement: If $X$ is Hausdorff and $K\subseteq X$ is compact, then $K$ is closed in $X$.
gloss: In a Hausdorff space, compactness forces closedness — a frequently used bridge between the two notions.
example: Combined with Thm. 6.37 it shows a continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is a homeomorphism.

statement: $A\subseteq X$ is \textbf{compact} if it is compact in the subspace topology; equivalently, every cover of $A$ by open subsets of $X$ has a finite subcollection covering $A$ (Lemma 6.34).
gloss: Compactness of a subset can be tested with open sets of the ambient space — often more convenient.
example: $S^n\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ is compact; the torus $T^2$ is compact.

statement: For topologies $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ on $X$: if $\mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2$ we say $\mathcal{T}_1$ is \textbf{coarser} and $\mathcal{T}_2$ is \textbf{finer}. They are \textbf{comparable} if one contains the other.
gloss: Topologies on a fixed set are partially ordered by inclusion. “Finer” means more open sets (more resolving power); “coarser” means fewer.
example: $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ for every $\mathcal{T}$. Not every pair of topologies is comparable.

statement: For $B\subseteq A$, the \textbf{complement} of $B$ in $A$ is $B^c=A\setminus B$.
gloss: The difference of $A$ and $B$ when an ambient set $A$ is understood. Openness and closedness in topology are dual via complements.
example: In a topological space, $K$ is closed iff $K^c$ is open.

statement: If $f\colon X\to Y$ and $g\colon Y\to Z$ are continuous, then $g\circ f\colon X\to Z$ is continuous.
gloss: Continuity is preserved under composition — the property that makes topological spaces and continuous maps into a category.
example: Used repeatedly, e.g. to show that being homeomorphic is transitive (Thm. 3.23).

statement: For $f\colon A\to B$ and $g\colon B\to C$, the \textbf{composition} $g\circ f\colon A\to C$ is $(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))$.
gloss: Apply $f$, then $g$. Composition is associative and the identity acts as a unit — the backbone of categorical thinking in topology.
example: $\mathrm{id}_B\circ f=f=f\circ\mathrm{id}_A$; continuity and homomorphism properties are preserved under composition.

statement: A \textbf{separation} of $X$ is a pair of disjoint non-empty open sets $U,V$ with $U\cup V=X$. $X$ is \textbf{connected} if it has no separation; otherwise \textbf{disconnected}.
gloss: Connected means the space cannot be split into two non-trivial open pieces — equivalently, its only clopen subsets are $\varnothing$ and $X$.
example: $\mathbb{R}$ is connected (Thm. 6.12); a two-point discrete space is disconnected.

statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous at $p$ iff for every neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$ there is a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ with $f(A)\subseteq B$.
gloss: Reformulates $\epsilon$-$\delta$ continuity purely with neighborhoods — the bridge that lets continuity make sense without a metric.
example: Recovers the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition by taking $B=\mathsf{B}_Y(f(p);\epsilon)$ and $A=\mathsf{B}_X(p;\delta)$.

statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ is \textbf{continuous at} $x$ if for every neighborhood $V$ of $f(x)$ there is a neighborhood $U$ of $x$ with $f(U)\subseteq V$.
gloss: Local continuity stated with neighborhoods. A map is continuous iff it is continuous at every point (Thm. 3.18).
example: Directly generalises the metric neighborhood criterion of Thm. 2.12.

statement: If $\mathcal{B}$ (resp. $\mathcal{S}$) is a basis (resp. subbasis) for $Y$, then $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff $f^{-1}(B)$ is open for every $B\in\mathcal{B}$ (resp. $f^{-1}(S)$ open for every $S\in\mathcal{S}$).
gloss: To check continuity you need only test preimages of basis (or subbasis) elements, not all open sets — a big practical economy.
example: Crucial for proving that projections out of a product are continuous.

statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ between metric spaces is continuous iff for every $B\subseteq Y$ open in $Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(B)$ is open in $X$.
gloss: The decisive insight: continuity is detected by preimages of open sets, with no reference to distances. This is what makes the leap to topological spaces possible.
example: This open-set criterion becomes the definition of continuity for topological spaces (Def. 3.11).

term: Continuous image of a compact space
statement: If $X$ is compact and $f\colon X\to Y$ is a surjective continuous map, then $Y$ is compact. Hence $f(X)$ is compact for any continuous $f$.
gloss: Continuity preserves compactness, just as it preserves connectedness. A key step toward the extreme value theorem.
example: Pull an open cover of $Y$ back through $f$, take a finite subcover upstairs, push forward.

term: Continuous image of a connected space
statement: If $X$ is connected, $Y$ a space, and $f\colon X\to Y$ a surjective continuous map, then $Y$ is connected. Hence $f(X)$ is connected for any continuous $f$.
gloss: Continuity cannot tear a connected space apart: its image stays in one piece. This makes connectedness a topological invariant.
example: Yields the Intermediate Value Theorem as a special case (Thm. 6.13).

statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ between metric spaces is \textbf{continuous at} $p$ if $\forall\,\epsilon>0\ \exists\,\delta>0$ such that $d_X(p,q)<\delta \Rightarrow d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\epsilon$. It is \textbf{continuous} if it is continuous at every $p\in X$.
gloss: The familiar $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition: points close in the domain are sent to points close in the codomain. Nearness is measured by the two metrics.
example: Every map out of a discrete metric space is continuous; the identity $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, but $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ is not.

statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ between topological spaces is \textbf{continuous} if for every open $V\subseteq Y$ the preimage $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$.
gloss: The definition of continuity in full generality: preimages of open sets are open. It needs only the open-set structure, no metric.
example: Constant maps are continuous (preimages are $\varnothing$ or $X$). Every map from a discrete space is continuous.

statement: A collection $\mathcal{A}$ of subsets is a \textbf{cover} of $X$ if $X=\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}}A$. It is an \textbf{open cover} if each $A$ is open. $\mathcal{A}'\subseteq\mathcal{A}$ is a \textbf{subcover} if it still covers.
gloss: A way of overlaying $X$ with (open) patches. The notion of “every open cover has a finite subcover” defines compactness.
example: Any basis or subbasis for $X$ is an open cover of $X$.

statement: A surjective continuous $p\colon E\to B$ is a \textbf{covering map} if every $b\in B$ has a neighborhood $U$ with $p^{-1}(U)=\bigsqcup_{\lambda}V_\lambda$ a disjoint union of opens, each mapped homeomorphically onto $U$ by $p$. $E$ \textbf{covers} $B$.
gloss: $B$ is “evenly covered”: locally $E$ looks like a stack of disjoint copies of a patch of $B$. The route to computing $\pi_1$ via lifting.
example: $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $t\mapsto e^{2\pi i t}$, is the universal cover of the circle.

statement: For $x\in G$ (multiplicative), $\langle x\rangle=\{x^n\mid n\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ is a subgroup — the \textbf{cyclic subgroup generated by} $x$, the smallest subgroup containing $x$. $G$ is \textbf{cyclic} if $G=\langle x'\rangle$ for some $x'$.
gloss: The powers of a single element form a subgroup. A whole group generated this way is cyclic; all cyclic groups are abelian.
example: $\mathbb{Z}=\langle 1\rangle=\langle -1\rangle$ is infinite cyclic. $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is cyclic.

statement: A homotopy $H\colon X\times I\to X$ is a \textbf{deformation retraction} onto $A$ if $H(x,0)=x$, $H(x,1)\in A$ for all $x$, and $H(a,t)=a$ for all $a\in A$, $t\in I$. Then $A$ is a \textbf{deformation retract}.
gloss: Continuously shrink $X$ onto $A$ over time, holding $A$ fixed throughout. It exhibits $X$ and $A$ as homotopy equivalent.
example: $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ deformation retracts onto $S^1$; a disc retracts onto its centre.

statement: $A\subseteq X$ is \textbf{dense} in $X$ if $\overline{A}=X$; equivalently, $A\cap U\neq\varnothing$ for every non-empty open $U\subseteq X$.
gloss: A dense set is everywhere — its closure fills the whole space, so it meets every open set.
example: $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$. In a discrete space the only dense subset is the whole space.

statement: For $B\subseteq A$, the \textbf{difference} is $A\setminus B=\{x\in A\mid x\notin B\}$.
gloss: What remains of $A$ after removing $B$. Disjoint from $B$, with $(A\setminus B)\cup B=A\cup B$.

statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{discrete topology} is $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}=\mathcal{P}(X)$, the power set: \textbf{every} subset is open.
gloss: The finest possible topology — a space of isolated points with no interaction. Singletons are open here, which uniquely characterises it.
example: For any set $X$, $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ is the largest topology; every map out of a discrete space is continuous.

statement: For a family $\{A_i\}_{i\in I}$, the \textbf{disjoint union} is $\bigsqcup_{i\in I}A_i=\bigcup_{i\in I}\{(x,i)\mid x\in A_i\}$ — elements are tagged pairs $(x,i)$.
gloss: Combine sets while keeping track of which piece each element came from, so copies never merge. Used to describe $p^{-1}(U)$ for covering maps.
example: The preimage of an evenly covered set is a disjoint union of homeomorphic sheets (Def. 8.1).

statement: For an equivalence relation $\sim$ on $X$, the \textbf{equivalence class} of $x$ is $[x]=\{y\in X\mid x\sim y\}$, and $X/\!\sim\ =\{[x]\mid x\in X\}$.
gloss: Classes partition $X$: any two are equal or disjoint, and together they cover $X$. The quotient set $X/\!\sim$ collects them.
example: Equivalence relations correspond (up to bijection) to surjective maps $X\to A$ (Lemma 5.15).

statement: For based maps $h_1\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ and $h_2\colon(Y,y_0)\to(Z,z_0)$: $(h_2\circ h_1)_*=(h_2)_*\circ(h_1)_*$, and $(\mathrm{id}_X)_*=\mathrm{id}$.
gloss: $\pi_1$ is a functor: it sends spaces to groups and maps to homomorphisms, respecting identities and composition.
example: Immediately gives that homeomorphic based spaces have isomorphic $\pi_1$ (Cor. 7.25).

statement: For a based space $(X,x_0)$, a \textbf{loop} at $x_0$ is a path from $x_0$ to $x_0$. The \textbf{fundamental group} is $\pi_1(X,x_0)=\{[f]\mid f\ \text{a loop at}\ x_0\}$ with operation $*$.
gloss: The group of loops at $x_0$ up to path homotopy. The identity is the constant loop and inverses are reversed loops. It measures “holes” the space cannot retract away.
example: $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^n,0)=0$ (trivial); $\pi_1(S^1,s_0)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ (Thm. 8.16).

statement: For a homomorphism $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$, the map $\bar\varphi\colon G_1/\ker\varphi\to\operatorname{im}\varphi$, $\bar\varphi(x\ker\varphi)=\varphi(x)$, is an isomorphism, and $\varphi=\bar\varphi\circ\pi$ where $\pi\colon G_1\to G_1/\ker\varphi$.
gloss: Any homomorphism factors as a quotient followed by an isomorphism: $G_1/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. The first isomorphism theorem.
example: Identifies the image of $\varphi$ with the quotient of $G_1$ by what $\varphi$ collapses.

statement: Every polynomial $z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_0$ of degree $n>0$ with complex coefficients has a complex root.
gloss: Complex polynomials always have roots — proved topologically via winding numbers of loops, i.e. via $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.
example: A non-vanishing polynomial would let a large loop be contracted, contradicting that its image winds $n$ times.

statement: If $X$ is compact and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then there exist $m,M\in X$ with $f(m)\le f(x)\le f(M)$ for all $x\in X$.
gloss: A continuous real function on a compact space attains its maximum and minimum. The image is compact, hence closed and bounded, hence contains its sup and inf.
example: The classical “continuous functions on $[a,b]$ attain extrema” is the case $X=[a,b]$.

statement: Let $X$ be connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. If $a,b\in X$ and $r$ lies between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then $f(c)=r$ for some $c\in X$.
gloss: A continuous real-valued function on a connected space hits every intermediate value — the classical IVT, freed from intervals.
example: If $r\notin f(X)$ then $f^{-1}((-\infty,r))$ and $f^{-1}((r,\infty))$ would separate $X$.

statement: A \textbf{group} is a non-empty set $G$ with a binary operation $*$ such that:\;\textbf{(G1)}\ associativity $(a*b)*c=a*(b*c)$;\;\textbf{(G2)}\ an identity $e$ with $a*e=a=e*a$;\;\textbf{(G3)}\ inverses $a'$ with $a*a'=e=a'*a$. It is \textbf{abelian} if $*$ is commutative.
gloss: A set with an associative, invertible operation and a neutral element. The algebraic target of the fundamental group functor.
example: $(\mathbb{Z},+)$ is an abelian group; $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is a group under $*$ (Thm. 7.16).

statement: A map $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$ is a \textbf{homomorphism} if $\varphi(x*_1 y)=\varphi(x)*_2\varphi(y)$ for all $x,y$.
gloss: A map respecting the group operations. Automatically $\varphi(e_1)=e_2$ and $\varphi(x^{-1})=\varphi(x)^{-1}$. Composites of homomorphisms are homomorphisms.
example: The induced map $h_*\colon\pi_1(X)\to\pi_1(Y)$ is a homomorphism (Def. 7.22).

statement: $X$ is \textbf{Hausdorff} ($T_2$) if for all distinct $x,y\in X$ there are disjoint open sets $U\ni x$ and $V\ni y$.
gloss: Distinct points can be “housed” in disjoint neighborhoods. This separation guarantees limits are unique and compact sets are closed.
example: Every metric space is Hausdorff; an infinite set with the cofinite topology is not.

statement: A subset $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ (Euclidean) is compact iff it is closed and bounded.
gloss: In Euclidean space, the abstract notion of compactness coincides exactly with the concrete “closed and bounded.” One of the cornerstones of analysis.
example: $[0,1]$, $S^n$, and closed balls are compact; open or unbounded sets are not.

statement: For a continuous bijection $f\colon X\to Y$, the following are equivalent: (1) $f$ is a homeomorphism; (2) $f$ is an open map; (3) $f$ is a closed map.
gloss: For a continuous bijection, having a continuous inverse is the same as being open, which is the same as being closed.
example: Often the quickest route to proving a continuous bijection is a homeomorphism (e.g. from a compact space to a Hausdorff one).

statement: A \textbf{homeomorphism} is a bijection $f\colon X\to Y$ such that both $f$ and $f^{-1}$ are continuous. If one exists we write $X\cong Y$ and call $X,Y$ \textbf{homeomorphic}.
gloss: The notion of “sameness” in topology: a continuous bijection with continuous inverse. It matches open sets of $X$ with open sets of $Y$ perfectly.
example: $f(x)=2x-1$ is a homeomorphism $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$. A continuous bijection need not be a homeomorphism (e.g. $\mathrm{id}\colon X_{\mathrm{disc}}\to X_{\mathrm{ind}}$).

statement: Continuous maps $f_0,f_1\colon X\to Y$ are \textbf{homotopic} ($f_0\simeq f_1$) if there is a continuous $H\colon X\times I\to Y$ with $H(x,0)=f_0(x)$ and $H(x,1)=f_1(x)$. $H$ is a \textbf{homotopy}.
gloss: A continuous one-parameter deformation of one map into another. Think of $t\in[0,1]$ as time, sliding $f_0$ to $f_1$.
example: If $f_0\simeq f_1$ with $f_1$ constant, $f_0$ is nullhomotopic. Any two maps into $\mathbb{R}^n$ are homotopic via the straight-line homotopy.

statement: On $C(X,Y)$ (continuous maps $X\to Y$), $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation. Its classes are \textbf{homotopy classes}; $[f]$ denotes the class of $f$ and $[X,Y]=C(X,Y)/\!\simeq$.
gloss: Bundle maps that can be deformed into one another. Topology often studies $[X,Y]$ rather than individual maps.
example: $[X,\mathbb{R}^n]$ has a single element, since all such maps are homotopic.

statement: Maps $f\colon X\to Y$, $g\colon Y\to X$ are \textbf{homotopy equivalences} if $g\circ f\simeq\mathrm{id}_X$ and $f\circ g\simeq\mathrm{id}_Y$; each is a \textbf{homotopy inverse} of the other.
gloss: A weaker sameness than homeomorphism: the spaces are interchangeable up to deformation. Every homeomorphism is a homotopy equivalence, but not conversely.
example: $\mathbb{R}$ and a point $\{p\}$ are homotopy equivalent but not homeomorphic.

statement: If $f\colon X\to Y$ is a homotopy equivalence with $f(x_0)=y_0$, then $f_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ is an isomorphism.
gloss: $\pi_1$ is an invariant of homotopy type, not merely of homeomorphism type — a stronger and very useful fact.
example: Lets you compute $\pi_1$ of a space via any simpler space it deformation retracts onto.

statement: Let $p\colon E\to B$ be a covering map with $p(e_0)=b_0$. Any continuous $H\colon I\times I\to B$ with $H(0,0)=b_0$ has a unique lift $\tilde H\colon I\times I\to E$ with $\tilde H(0,0)=e_0$; if $H$ is a path homotopy so is $\tilde H$.
gloss: Whole homotopies lift uniquely, and path homotopies lift to path homotopies. This makes the lifting correspondence well-defined on $\pi_1$.
example: Ensures path-homotopic loops in $B$ have lifts with the same endpoint, so $\Phi$ is well-defined (Def. 8.14).

statement: Spaces $X,Y$ have the same \textbf{homotopy type} (are \textbf{homotopy equivalent}) if there is a homotopy equivalence $f\colon X\to Y$.
gloss: The equivalence relation under which topology classifies spaces ‘up to deformation’. Many invariants, including $\pi_1$, depend only on homotopy type.
example: All contractible spaces (those homotopy equivalent to a point) share one homotopy type.

statement: For $f\colon A\to B$, the \textbf{image} is $f(A)=\{f(x)\in B\mid x\in A\}\subseteq B$.
gloss: All values actually attained by $f$. $f$ is surjective exactly when $f(A)=B$.
example: For $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, the image is $[0,\infty)$.

statement: For $R\subseteq A$, the \textbf{inclusion} $i\colon R\to A$ is $i(x)=x$.
gloss: The map that views a subset as sitting inside the ambient set. Composing with it recovers a restriction: $f\circ i=f|_R$.
example: The inclusion of a subspace is continuous for the subspace topology.

statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{indiscrete topology} is $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}=\{\varnothing,X\}$.
gloss: The coarsest possible topology — the only open sets are the empty set and the whole space. Points are topologically indistinguishable.
example: For any topology $\mathcal{T}$ on $X$ we have $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$. Every map into an indiscrete space is continuous.

statement: A based map $h\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ induces $h_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ by $h_*([f])=[h\circ f]$; it is a group homomorphism (Lemma 7.23).
gloss: Maps of spaces turn into maps of fundamental groups by post-composing loops. This is how $\pi_1$ becomes algebraic.
example: $(\mathrm{id}_X)_*$ is the identity; composites behave well (functoriality, Thm. 7.24).

statement: The \textbf{interior} $\mathrm{Int}(A)$ is the union of all open subsets of $X$ contained in $A$ — the largest open set inside $A$.
gloss: Dual to the closure: it strips $A$ down to the points it surrounds with room to spare. Always $\mathrm{Int}(A)\subseteq A\subseteq\overline{A}$.
example: In $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathrm{Int}((a,b])=(a,b)$.

statement: $A\cap B=\{x\mid x\in A\ \text{and}\ x\in B\}$ and $A\cup B=\{x\mid x\in A\ \text{or}\ x\in B\}$ (inclusive “or”).
gloss: Intersection keeps common elements; union pools both. They are commutative, associative, and distribute over each other.
example: $A,B$ are disjoint when $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Always $A\cap B\subseteq A\subseteq A\cup B$.

statement: If $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ are topologies on $X$, then $\mathcal{T}_1\cap\mathcal{T}_2$ is a topology. More generally $\bigcap_{\lambda}\mathcal{T}_\lambda$ is a topology for any family.
gloss: Topologies are closed under intersection — the basis for “generating” the smallest topology containing a given collection. The union of topologies generally is not a topology.
example: On $\{a,b,c\}$, intersecting two topologies can give $\{\varnothing,X\}$ while their union fails T2.

statement: An \textbf{isomorphism} is a bijective homomorphism $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$. An isomorphism from a group to itself is an \textbf{automorphism}.
gloss: A perfect structural match between groups: same group, relabelled. Isomorphic groups are interchangeable for all group-theoretic purposes.
example: $\Phi\colon\pi_1(S^1)\to\mathbb{Z}$ is an isomorphism (Thm. 8.16).

statement: For a compact metric space $(X,d)$ and an open cover $\mathcal{A}$, there is $\lambda>0$ (a \textbf{Lebesgue number}) such that every ball $\mathsf{B}(x;\lambda)$ lies inside some single $U\in\mathcal{A}$.
gloss: On a compact metric space, every open cover has a uniform scale below which small balls fit inside one cover element. Enables subdividing paths finely enough to lift.
example: Used to chop $I$ into small subintervals, each mapping into one evenly covered open set (proof of Thm. 8.12).

statement: For $H\le G$ and $g\in G$: the \textbf{left coset} is $gH=\{g*h\mid h\in H\}$ and the \textbf{right coset} is $Hg=\{h*g\mid h\in H\}$.
gloss: Translates of a subgroup. Cosets partition $G$ into equal-sized blocks; they coincide ($gH=Hg$) precisely for normal subgroups.
example: In an abelian group all left and right cosets agree.

statement: Given $p\colon E\to B$ and $f\colon X\to B$, a \textbf{lifting} of $f$ is a continuous $\tilde f\colon X\to E$ with $p\circ\tilde f=f$.
gloss: Lift a map from the base into the total space, compatibly with $p$. Path and homotopy lifting (for covering maps) drive the computation of $\pi_1(S^1)$.
example: A path in $S^1$ lifts to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ once a starting point is chosen (Thm. 8.12).

statement: For a covering $p\colon E\to B$ with $p(e_0)=b_0$, the \textbf{lifting correspondence} is $\Phi\colon\pi_1(B,b_0)\to p^{-1}(b_0)$, $\Phi([f])=\tilde f(1)$, where $\tilde f$ is the lift of the loop $f$ with $\tilde f(0)=e_0$.
gloss: Send each loop class to the endpoint of its lift in the fibre. Well-defined by homotopy lifting; a bijection when $E$ is path-connected (Thm. 8.15).
example: For $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $\Phi$ sends a loop to its winding number, giving $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ is a \textbf{local homeomorphism} if every $x\in X$ has a neighborhood $U$ with $f(U)$ open and $f|_U\colon U\to f(U)$ a homeomorphism.
gloss: Looks like a homeomorphism in a neighborhood of each point, though not necessarily globally. Every covering map is a local homeomorphism.
example: Preserves local properties such as local connectedness; $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ is a local homeomorphism but not injective.

statement: A \textbf{metric space} $(X,d)$ is a non-empty set $X$ with a map $d\colon X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ such that for all $x,y,z\in X$:\;\textbf{(M1)}\ d(x,y)\ge 0,\ \text{and}\ d(x,y)=0\Leftrightarrow x=y;\;\textbf{(M2)}\ d(x,y)=d(y,x);\;\textbf{(M3)}\ d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z).
gloss: A set equipped with a notion of distance. The three axioms say distance is non-negative (and zero only between identical points), symmetric, and obeys the triangle inequality.
example: $\mathbb{R}^n$ with $d(x,y)=\lVert x-y\rVert$ is the prototype. The discrete metric ($d(x,y)=1$ for $x\neq y$, else $0$) makes any set a metric space.

statement: $A\subseteq X$ is a \textbf{neighborhood} of $x$ if some open ball about $x$ is contained in $A$. It is an \textbf{open neighborhood} if $A$ itself is open.
gloss: A set that surrounds $x$ with some breathing room. It need not be open itself — only contain an open ball around $x$.
example: In $\mathbb{R}$, the interval $[-1,2]$ is a neighborhood of $0$ but not of the endpoint $2$.

statement: For a topological space $X$ and $x\in X$, a subset $U$ is a \textbf{neighborhood} of $x$ if $x\in U$ and $U$ is open in $X$.
gloss: In these notes a neighborhood is simply an open set containing the point (an “open neighborhood”). A set is open iff it is a neighborhood of each of its points (Thm. 3.10).
example: In $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology, $(-1,1)$ is a neighborhood of $0$.

statement: $H\le G$ is \textbf{normal} if $gH=Hg$ for all $g$; equivalently $g*h*g^{-1}\in H$ for all $g\in G$, $h\in H$.
gloss: A subgroup whose left and right cosets agree — exactly the condition needed for the cosets to form a group.
example: Every subgroup of an abelian group is normal; kernels of homomorphisms are always normal.

statement: A map $f$ is \textbf{nullhomotopic} if $f\simeq c$ for some constant map $c$.
gloss: The map can be continuously shrunk to a single point. Loops being nullhomotopic is what makes a space simply connected.
example: Every map into $\mathbb{R}^n$ is nullhomotopic; the identity $S^1\to S^1$ is not.

statement: $A\subseteq X$ is \textbf{open} if every $a\in A$ has an open ball $\mathsf{B}(a;r)\subseteq A$. It is \textbf{closed} if its complement $A^c=X\setminus A$ is open.
gloss: Open means every point sits inside the set with a little room to spare. Closed is defined by its complement. Most sets are neither; sets that are both are called clopen.
example: Open balls are open and closed balls are closed (Lemma 2.10). Both $\varnothing$ and $X$ are clopen.

statement: For $(X,d)$, $a\in X$ and $r>0$: the \textbf{open ball} is $\mathsf{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\}$ and the \textbf{closed ball} is $\overline{\mathsf{B}}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\le r\}$.
gloss: All points within distance $r$ of a centre $a$ — strictly less than $r$ for open, at most $r$ for closed. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ these are ordinary balls and discs.
example: In the discrete metric, $\mathsf{B}(x;r)=\{x\}$ for $r\le 1$ and $\mathsf{B}(x;r)=X$ for $r>1$.

statement: A continuous $f\colon X\to Y$ is an \textbf{open map} if $f(U)$ is open for every open $U$, and a \textbf{closed map} if $f(V)$ is closed for every closed $V$.
gloss: These push openness/closedness forward, unlike continuity which pulls openness back. They are the tool linking quotient maps to homeomorphisms.
example: Every homeomorphism is both open and closed, but $\mathbb{R}\to\{*\}$ is open and closed yet not a homeomorphism.

statement: Let $X=A\cup B$ with $A,B$ closed in $X$, and let $f\colon A\to Y$, $g\colon B\to Y$ be continuous with $f=g$ on $A\cap B$. Then $h\colon X\to Y$ defined piecewise by $f$ and $g$ is continuous.
gloss: Two continuous pieces that agree on the overlap glue to a continuous whole — provided the pieces are closed.
example: Makes the concatenation of paths $f*g$ continuous (Def. 7.12).

statement: Paths $f,g\colon I\to X$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ are \textbf{path homotopic} ($f\simeq_p g$) if there is continuous $H\colon I\times I\to X$ with $H(s,0)=f(s)$, $H(s,1)=g(s)$, and $H(0,t)=x_0$, $H(1,t)=x_1$ for all $t$.
gloss: Deform one path into another while keeping the endpoints pinned. This rel-endpoints relation is what the fundamental group is built from.
example: Any two paths in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the same endpoints are path homotopic via the straight-line homotopy.

statement: For a path $f$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$, $[f]=\{g\mid g\ \text{a path}\ x_0\to x_1,\ f\simeq_p g\}$ is its \textbf{path homotopy class}.
gloss: The endpoint-fixed deformation class of a path. The fundamental group consists of such classes for loops.
example: The product $*$ and inverse descend to these classes, giving group structure (Thm. 7.14).

statement: A \textbf{path} from $x$ to $y$ is a continuous map $f\colon[a,b]\to X$ with $f(a)=x$, $f(b)=y$. $X$ is \textbf{path-connected} if any two points are joined by a path.
gloss: You can walk continuously between any two points. A stronger, more geometric cousin of connectedness.
example: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is path-connected, as is $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{p\}$ for $n\ge 2$.

statement: Every path-connected space is connected.
gloss: Being walkable implies being in one piece. The converse fails in general, though it holds for open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$.
example: The topologist's sine curve is connected but not path-connected (a standard counterexample).

term: Product of compact spaces is compact
statement: If $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are compact, then $X_1\times\cdots\times X_n$ is compact.
gloss: Compactness survives finite products (and, by Tychonoff, arbitrary products with the product topology).
example: With Thm. 6.40 this gives compactness of closed boxes in $\mathbb{R}^n$, hence Heine–Borel.

statement: For a path $f$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ and $g$ from $x_1$ to $x_2$, the \textbf{product} $f*g\colon I\to X$ is $f$ run on $[0,\tfrac12]$ then $g$ on $[\tfrac12,1]$: $(f*g)(s)=f(2s)$ for $s\le\tfrac12$, $g(2s-1)$ otherwise.
gloss: Concatenate two paths by traversing the first at double speed, then the second. Continuous by the pasting lemma.
example: On path homotopy classes, $*$ is associative with constant-path units and reverse-path inverses (Thm. 7.14).

statement: For spaces $X,Y$, the \textbf{product topology} on $X\times Y$ is generated by the basis $\mathcal{B}=\{U\times V\mid U\ \text{open in}\ X,\ V\ \text{open in}\ Y\}$.
gloss: Open sets of the product are unions of open rectangles. It is the coarsest topology making both projections continuous (its universal property).
example: If $\mathcal{B}_X,\mathcal{B}_Y$ are bases, the products $B_X\times B_Y$ form a basis (Thm. 5.9). The torus $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ carries this topology.

statement: For a normal subgroup $H\trianglelefteq G$, the cosets $G/H$ form a group under $(g_1H)(g_2H)=(g_1g_2)H$.
gloss: Collapse a normal subgroup to the identity: the cosets inherit a well-defined group structure. The algebraic analogue of a quotient space.
example: $\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}$ is the cyclic group of order $m$.

statement: For a surjection $\pi\colon X\to A$ from a space $X$, the \textbf{quotient topology} on $A$ is $\{U\subseteq A\mid \pi^{-1}(U)\ \text{open in}\ X\}$. Then $\pi$ is a \textbf{quotient map} and $A$ the \textbf{quotient space}.
gloss: Glue points of $X$ together (via $\pi$) and declare a set in $A$ open exactly when its preimage is open. It is the finest topology making $\pi$ continuous.
example: Collapsing the boundary circle of a disc to a point yields a sphere; gluing the ends of $[0,1]$ yields $S^1$.

statement: For $f\colon A\to B$ and $R\subseteq A$, the \textbf{restriction} $f|_R\colon R\to B$ is $f|_R(x)=f(x)$ for $x\in R$.
gloss: Use the same rule on a smaller domain. Restrictions appear constantly, e.g. $p|_{V_\lambda}$ in covering maps.
example: If $f|_R$ is injective, then $f|_R\colon R\to f(R)$ is a bijection.

statement: For a subspace $A\subseteq X$, a continuous $r\colon X\to A$ is a \textbf{retraction} if $r(a)=a$ for all $a\in A$. Then $A$ is a \textbf{retract} of $X$.
gloss: A continuous way of collapsing $X$ onto $A$ that leaves $A$ fixed. Retractions constrain how $\pi_1(A)$ sits inside $\pi_1(X)$.
example: $S^1$ is a retract of $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ via $r(x)=x/\lVert x\rVert$.

statement: A path-connected space $X$ is \textbf{simply connected} if $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is trivial for some (hence every) $x_0\in X$.
gloss: Path-connected and every loop contracts to a point — no $1$-dimensional holes.
example: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is simply connected; $S^1$ and the torus $T^2$ are not.

statement: A \textbf{subbasis} on $X$ is a collection $\mathcal{S}$ of subsets whose union is $X$. Its finite intersections form a basis, whose unions form the \textbf{topology generated by} $\mathcal{S}$.
gloss: An even smaller seed: take finite intersections to get a basis, then unions to get the topology. Open sets are unions of finite intersections of subbasis elements.
example: $\langle\mathcal{S}\rangle=\big\{\bigcup_\lambda\bigcap_{i=1}^{n_\lambda}S_{\lambda,i}\mid S_{\lambda,i}\in\mathcal{S}\big\}$ (Thm. 4.15).

statement: A subset $H\subseteq G$ is a \textbf{subgroup} ($H\le G$) if $*$ restricts to a binary operation on $H$ making $(H,*|_{H\times H})$ a group. It is \textbf{proper} ($H<G$) if $H\subsetneq G$.
gloss: A subset that is itself a group under the same operation. The trivial subgroup $\{e\}$ always qualifies.
example: $m\mathbb{Z}=\{mn\mid n\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ is a proper subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$ for $m\ge 2$.

statement: $B$ is a \textbf{subset} of $A$ ($B\subseteq A$) if every element of $B$ is an element of $A$. If also $A\neq B$, then $B$ is a \textbf{proper subset}, $B\subsetneq A$.
gloss: Everything in $B$ is already in $A$. Sets are equal iff each is a subset of the other.
example: $\{1,2\}\subsetneq\{1,2,3\}$ and $\mathbb{Z}\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.

statement: For $A\subseteq X$, the \textbf{subspace topology} on $A$ is $\mathcal{T}_A=\{A\cap U\mid U\ \text{open in}\ X\}$.
gloss: $A$ inherits openness by slicing the open sets of $X$. A set may be open in $A$ without being open in $X$.
example: $K\subseteq A$ is closed in $A$ iff $K=A\cap L$ for some $L$ closed in $X$ (Thm. 5.3).

statement: $f\colon A\to B$ is \textbf{surjective} if every $b\in B$ equals $f(a)$ for some $a$; \textbf{injective} if $f(a_1)=f(a_2)\Rightarrow a_1=a_2$; \textbf{bijective} if both.
gloss: Surjective hits everything, injective never collides, bijective is a perfect one-to-one matching. A bijection has an inverse $f^{-1}$.
example: $f$ is bijective iff there is $g$ with $g\circ f=\mathrm{id}_A$ and $f\circ g=\mathrm{id}_B$ (Thm. A.10).

statement: $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology is connected.
gloss: The fundamental connected space. The proof uses the least-upper-bound property of $\mathbb{R}$.
example: Consequently all intervals are connected, and $\mathbb{R}^n$ is connected.

statement: A \textbf{topological property} is any property expressible in terms of points and open sets; such properties are preserved by homeomorphisms. A \textbf{topological invariant} is a quantity invariant under homeomorphism.
gloss: These are exactly the features topology can see. If $X\cong Y$ and $X$ has the property, so does $Y$ — hence invariants can prove two spaces are not homeomorphic.
example: Connectedness, compactness, Hausdorffness and the fundamental group are all topological invariants.

statement: A \textbf{topological space} is a set $X$ with a collection $\mathcal{T}$ of subsets (the \textbf{open sets}) such that:\;\textbf{(T1)}\ \varnothing,X\in\mathcal{T};\;\textbf{(T2)}\ arbitrary unions of members of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$;\;\textbf{(T3)}\ finite intersections of members of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$.
gloss: The central object: a set together with a choice of which subsets count as open. Note the asymmetry — any union but only finite intersections.
example: Every metric space is a topological space via its metric topology (Thm. 3.2). On $\{a,b,c\}$ there are $29$ distinct topologies.

statement: For a collection $\mathcal{S}$ of subsets of $X$, the \textbf{topology generated by} $\mathcal{S}$ is $\langle\mathcal{S}\rangle=\bigcap\{\mathcal{T}\ \text{topology}\mid \mathcal{S}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\}$, the smallest topology containing $\mathcal{S}$.
gloss: Take any collection of “intended open sets” and form the coarsest topology making them open, by intersecting all topologies that contain them.
example: $\langle\varnothing\rangle=\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}$; the collection of all singletons generates $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$.

statement: Let $Y$ be compact. If $x\in X$ and $U$ is open in $X\times Y$ containing the slice $\{x\}\times Y$, then there is a neighborhood $W$ of $x$ with $W\times Y\subseteq U$.
gloss: An open set containing a whole compact slice automatically contains a “tube” $W\times Y$ around it. The technical engine behind compactness of products.
example: Used directly to prove that a finite product of compact spaces is compact (Thm. 6.39).

statement: Let $p\colon E\to B$ be a covering map with $p(e_0)=b_0$. For any path $f\colon I\to B$ with $f(0)=b_0$ there is a \textbf{unique} path $\tilde f\colon I\to E$ lifting $f$ with $\tilde f(0)=e_0$.
gloss: Paths lift uniquely once you fix a starting point upstairs. The cornerstone for relating loops in $B$ to points in a fibre.
example: For $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, a loop winding once lifts to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ from $0$ to $1$.

statement: The quotient topology is the unique topology on $A$ such that for every space $Y$ and every map $f\colon A\to Y$: $f$ is continuous iff $f\circ\pi\colon X\to Y$ is continuous.
gloss: A clean test: a map out of a quotient is continuous exactly when its composite with $\pi$ is. This characterises the quotient topology completely.
example: Lets you define continuous maps on glued spaces by defining them on $X$ in a $\sim$-compatible way.

statement: If $h\colon X\to Y$ is a homeomorphism with $h(x_0)=y_0$, then $h_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ is an isomorphism.
gloss: Homeomorphic spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups — so different $\pi_1$ proves spaces are not homeomorphic.
example: Since $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)=0$ but $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$, the plane and the circle are not homeomorphic.

statement: For based spaces $(X,x_0),(Y,y_0)$: $\pi_1(X\times Y,(x_0,y_0))\cong\pi_1(X,x_0)\times\pi_1(Y,y_0)$.
gloss: The fundamental group of a product is the direct product of the fundamental groups — loops split into their coordinate loops.
example: $\pi_1(T^2)=\pi_1(S^1\times S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$.

statement: For any $s_0\in S^1$, the lifting correspondence $\Phi\colon\pi_1(S^1,s_0)\to\mathbb{Z}$ is a group isomorphism.
gloss: The fundamental group of the circle is the integers, with the integer counting how many times a loop winds around. The first non-trivial $\pi_1$ computation.
example: It underlies the $2$-dimensional Brouwer theorem and the fundamental theorem of algebra.
